goals.g1: What order must the compound orchestrator resolve matches' output_maps in, and what does it do when a redirect's owner disposed of the shared node with no surviving output?

c1.statement: a redirect entry (i, out_node_i) -> owner means we need owner's resolved output_map -- specifically which of owner's own output nodes inherits from whichever input was bound to the shared real node

c2.statement: output_map for a match can only be computed once its own forced_born overrides are known AND once any match it depends on via its own redirects is already resolved -- redirect edges define a dependency: i must resolve AFTER owner(i)

c3.statement: in all 4 tests run so far, no owner was itself a crosser, so a naive fixed order was sufficient and not chain-stress-tested

c5.statement: if owner's core.inherit has no output claiming the input bound to the shared real node, that node has no surviving identity after owner resolves -- the crossing target genuinely ceased to exist and must be DROPPED, not reattached to the stale node

c6.statement: dropping a crossing to a deleted target matches the framework's language of routing SURVIVING identity, never preserving a connection to a node no output represents anymore

c4.statement: DECISION: order via topological sort of the dependency graph (edge owner->i for each redirect (i,_)->owner); cycle detection raises rather than guessing. When owner's inherit has no claim on the shared node, drop the redirected crossing instead of reattaching to the stale pre-fire identity.

derived_from.rule: topo sort generalizes the already-validated fixed order to arbitrary chain depth without new per-depth logic; cycles are a real (if pathological) reachable case per redirect's own definition, so failing loudly beats guessing

ans.statement: Order via topological sort of the redirect dependency graph, cycle detection raises. Drop redirected crossings whose owner has no surviving inherit claim on the shared node.

k1.method: does topo-sort reduce to the fixed order already validated in tests 1-4 with no chain?
k1.result: yes -- owner->crosser edges only, no owner-of-owner edges, topo sort trivially reproduces the already-validated order

k2.method: check whether a redirect cycle is reachable given detect_overlaps/resolve_compound's actual contract, or definitionally impossible
k2.result: reachable in principle (X crosses into Y's region and Y crosses into X's region in the same firing) -- not excluded by the current contract, so cycle detection is live code, not dead code

committed.statement: DECISION: order via topological sort of the dependency graph (edge owner->i for each redirect (i,_)->owner); cycle detection raises rather than guessing. When owner's inherit has no claim on the shared node, drop the redirected crossing instead of reattaching to the stale pre-fire identity.
